card.title: Camus and the Absurd
card.teaser: A man pushes a boulder up a mountain forever. Camus said we should imagine him happy — and meant it literally.
card.coverImagePrompt: A lone figure pushing a massive stone up a stark Mediterranean mountain at dusk, long shadow behind him, warm amber sky, painterly and still

lessons.title: Is life worth living?
lessons.body: That's the first sentence — more or less — of Albert Camus's 1942 essay The Myth of Sisyphus. He called it the only truly serious philosophical question. Everything else, he argued, is detail. And his answer wasn't a reassurance. It was a dare.

visual.imagePrompt: A solitary cafe table in 1940s Paris with an open notebook and a cigarette burning low, rain on the windows, noir atmosphere

lessons.title: What 'the Absurd' means to Camus
lessons.body: The Absurd, for Camus, isn't 'silly.' It's the collision between two things: the human mind's deep hunger for meaning, and a universe that refuses to supply any. We crave purpose; reality shrugs. That gap is the Absurd. It isn't out in the world, and it isn't in us alone — it's the friction between.

keyTerms.definition: The conflict between human beings' search for meaning and a silent, indifferent universe
French-Algerian writer and philosopher (1913–1960), Nobel laureate 1957

visual.imagePrompt: A human silhouette shouting questions into a vast starry sky that simply hangs there, blue-black cosmic calm

lessons.title: Three bad answers and one good one
lessons.body: Camus outlines three common responses to the Absurd. First: physical suicide — ending the question by ending yourself. Second: 'philosophical suicide' — pretending the gap isn't there by accepting a religion or ideology that fills it. Third: hopeless despair. Camus rejects all three. His answer is to face the Absurd clearly and keep living anyway — what he calls revolt.

keyTerms.definition: Camus's term for taking a leap of faith into any ideology that papers over the Absurd
The defiant choice to live fully in full awareness of the Absurd, without pretending it's resolved

visual.imagePrompt: A single candle burning stubbornly in a wide dark room, a window with a storm outside, soft flickering shadows

lessons.title: Why Sisyphus is happy
lessons.body: The Greek myth: Sisyphus is condemned to roll a boulder up a mountain for eternity, watching it roll back down every time. Camus uses him as the image of every human life — effort without ultimate meaning. But he ends the essay with a line that shocked readers in 1942 and still does: 'One must imagine Sisyphus happy.' Happy, because in the moment he walks back down the mountain, he owns the rock. He's defied the gods by refusing to despair.
lessons.funFact: Camus wrote the essay while working in the French Resistance and living under Nazi occupation — the question 'is life worth living?' wasn't abstract.

visual.imagePrompt: A painted scene of Sisyphus walking back down a mountain, shoulders relaxed, a faint private smile, golden dust

lessons.title: Meaning is the walk back down
lessons.body: Camus wasn't a nihilist, despite the misreading. He believed that once you stop demanding meaning from the universe, you become free to make some — in the specific, temporary, deeply human ways available to you. The point isn't to solve the Absurd. It's to keep showing up, keep loving, keep creating, even when none of it adds up. Especially then.

quiz.question: For Camus, what is 'the Absurd'?
options: The tension between our need for meaning and a silent universe
The belief that nothing is real
quiz.explanation: The Absurd is the collision between the human craving for meaning and a universe that refuses to provide it.

quiz.question: Which response to the Absurd does Camus endorse?
options: Conscious revolt — living fully in full awareness of the Absurd
quiz.explanation: Camus calls his answer 'revolt' — confronting the Absurd honestly and continuing to live and create anyway.

quiz.question: How does Camus end The Myth of Sisyphus?
options: With the line 'One must imagine Sisyphus happy'
quiz.explanation: The essay closes with the deliberately jarring claim that Sisyphus, despite his eternal task, must be imagined as happy — owning his fate through revolt.

sources.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
sources.publisher: Albert Camus, translated by Justin O'Brien, Vintage International
